summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions on topics ranging from dating strategies for introverts, career paths in data science without grad school, mentoring first-generation college students, finding ethical employment after leaving the military, and the ethics of giving money to panhandlers. O'Neil provides practical, often contrarian advice grounded in her experiences as a mathematician and data scientist, while weaving in observations about how organizations and systems operate. The column showcases her characteristic blend of warmth, directness, and systemic thinking.
key_points: Most research doesn't pay for itself, so companies won't hire people to do abstract research regardless of credentials - even PhDs need to tie their work to revenue streams
Once you're sensitized to organizational ethics, you'll see problems almost everywhere that pays well, from tech giants to startups
The people who need help most aren't the ones asking for petty cash in Penn Station - poverty is a systemic issue that requires addressing root causes
First-generation and low-income students benefit from explicit offers of help and positive feedback, but sharing your own nonlinear path works better one-on-one after trust is established
Nerd communities like programming meetups can be good places to meet introverted partners, but patience is required to draw people out
money_quotes: I'ma be honest with you, most research doesn't pay for itself. Indeed it's pretty rare for research to pay off.

The truth is, once you've been politicized and sensitized to the evil that organizations do or are involved it, you start to see it everywhere. Or if not everywhere, at least most places where you get paid.
Working at Google might not be a good idea for you since we don't really know what they do.
I never give scary men money because they're threatening me, whether they're black or white, on principle.
Having worked in soup kitchens and having read enough about childhood poverty and hunger, I know that the people who need petty cash the most aren't the ones asking for it in Penn Station.
Start-ups often participate in weird shit in ways they don't want to acknowledge.
I can't take any money with me when I die, and that I expect to die with more than 12 bucks in my name, so in the end it doesn't matter.
key_insight: Ethical concerns about organizations and systems are legitimate but create real tradeoffs - recognizing systemic problems means accepting that most well-paying work involves some complicity, so the question becomes how to navigate that reality rather than escape it entirely.
